course_info.title: Brand Communication and Identity
prerequisites: DC201 - Social Media Strategy and Management

class_schedule.topic: Brand Identity Systems in the Digital Age
learning_objectives: Define brand identity and distinguish it from brand image and brand reputation
Identify the components of a comprehensive digital brand identity system
Evaluate how digital channels have transformed brand building from broadcast to conversation

lecture_notes.introduction: Brand communication is where strategy meets expression. In digital environments, every post, email, and interaction contributes to or erodes brand identity. This course teaches you to build, maintain, and evolve brand identity systems that are consistent across channels and resilient under pressure.
key_concepts: Brand identity: the outward expression a brand creates (visual, verbal, experiential)
Brand image: how the audience perceives the brand, which may differ from intended identity
Digital brand touchpoints: website, social profiles, email, apps, customer service, advertising
Brand architecture: how sub-brands and product lines relate to the parent brand
Voice and tone systems: consistent personality expressed differently across contexts
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative analysis of three brands with strong digital identities (Apple, Mailchimp, Glossier), mapping their visual, verbal, and experiential consistency across channels
setup_requirements: Access to major social platforms for brand observation

review.value: Identity is what the brand projects; image is what the audience receives. The gap between the two reveals communication failures or external perception challenges that strategy must address

Voice is the brand's consistent personality (e.g., friendly, authoritative). Tone varies by context (celebratory in a launch post, empathetic in a support response). Voice stays constant; tone adapts.

class_schedule.topic: Developing Brand Voice and Messaging Frameworks
learning_objectives: Create a brand voice document with personality attributes, do/don't guidelines, and examples
Develop a messaging hierarchy from tagline through value propositions to proof points
Apply voice and messaging frameworks to real content creation across channels

lecture_notes.introduction: A brand without a defined voice sounds different every time someone new writes for it. A messaging framework without a voice document produces technically correct but emotionally flat content. Both together create communications that are recognizable, consistent, and human.
key_concepts: Brand personality spectrum: defining where the brand falls on dimensions like formal/casual, serious/playful, traditional/innovative
Voice documentation: personality attributes, each with a description, do's, don'ts, and content examples
Messaging hierarchy: mission statement, tagline, value propositions, proof points, and how they nest
Channel adaptation: maintaining voice while adapting tone, length, and format for different platforms
Voice governance: onboarding new team members and maintaining consistency at scale
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete brand voice document for a fictional direct-to-consumer brand: defining three personality attributes, writing do/don't guidelines, and applying the voice to five different content pieces

review.value: A nested framework: mission (why we exist), tagline (memorable expression of value), value propositions (specific benefits), proof points (evidence supporting each proposition); all content should trace back to this hierarchy
The systems and processes that maintain brand voice consistency when multiple people create content: voice documents, editorial reviews, content templates, and onboarding training

class_schedule.topic: Visual Brand Systems for Digital Channels
learning_objectives: Design a visual brand system including color, typography, imagery, and layout rules
Create branded templates for social media, email, and presentation formats
Ensure visual consistency across responsive and multi-platform environments

lecture_notes.introduction: Visual brand systems are more than logos and color palettes. They are comprehensive frameworks ensuring every visual touchpoint reinforces brand recognition and communicates brand personality, from a social media story to a corporate presentation.
key_concepts: Color systems: primary, secondary, accent, and semantic colors with accessibility ratios
Typography systems: heading and body typefaces, scale, weight usage, and platform fallbacks
Image and illustration style: photography direction, illustration style, icon systems
Layout grids and spacing: consistent structure that adapts across screen sizes
Brand asset management: organizing, distributing, and governing brand assets at scale
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete digital brand kit in Figma: defining color tokens, typography scale, component library, and three social media templates that demonstrate system consistency

review.value: Named values (colors, spacing, typography) that are referenced throughout a design system rather than hard-coded; changing a token value updates every instance, enabling brand evolution without rebuilding individual assets

All color pairings must meet WCAG 2.1 contrast ratios (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text); brand colors that fail accessibility cannot be used for text, requiring accessible alternatives in the system

learning_objectives: Develop a brand narrative that connects organizational purpose to audience aspirations
Create story-driven content campaigns that reinforce brand positioning
Apply narrative techniques to corporate communications including about pages and investor communications
readings: Sachs, Winning the Story Wars, Ch. 1-5
Simon Sinek, Start with Why, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Brands that tell compelling stories build emotional connections that survive product changes, competitive pressure, and market shifts. Brand storytelling is not about making things up. It is about finding and articulating the true stories that reveal what the brand stands for.
key_concepts: Origin story: the founding narrative that establishes brand authenticity and purpose
Customer success stories: making the customer the hero, with the brand as enabler
Cultural narrative: connecting the brand to larger social or cultural movements
Episodic brand content: serialized storytelling that builds audience anticipation and loyalty
Authentic storytelling: the difference between genuine narrative and manufactured mythology
lecture_notes.demonstration: Deconstructing three brand origin stories (Patagonia, Airbnb, Warby Parker) to identify narrative structure, then drafting an origin story for a student's hypothetical brand

review.value: A narrative of founding that establishes purpose, values, and authenticity; effective origin stories reveal the problem the founder experienced, the insight that sparked the solution, and the values that guided the journey
Sinek's framework: Why (purpose), How (process), What (product); brands that communicate from the inside out (starting with why) build deeper connections than those that lead with what they sell

learning_objectives: Define brand equity and identify measurable indicators of brand health
Design a brand tracking study using surveys, social listening, and search data
Interpret brand health metrics to inform strategic communications decisions
readings: Brandwatch, 'Social Listening for Brand Health' guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Brand is one of the most valuable yet hardest to measure business assets. Brand communication professionals must be able to demonstrate the impact of their work through rigorous measurement of brand awareness, perception, and equity over time.
key_concepts: Brand equity components: awareness, associations, perceived quality, loyalty
Measurement methods: brand tracking surveys, social listening, share of voice, branded search volume
Net Promoter Score (NPS): measuring brand advocacy through likelihood to recommend
Social sentiment analysis: tracking how people feel about your brand in public conversations
Brand health dashboard: combining quantitative and qualitative metrics for ongoing monitoring
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a brand health dashboard combining branded search volume (Google Trends), social listening sentiment (Brandwatch demo), NPS survey data, and share of voice calculations

review.value: The percentage of total industry conversation or visibility that your brand occupies, measured across channels (social mentions, search impressions, media coverage); correlates with market share over time
The number of people searching for your brand name directly; an increase indicates growing brand awareness, while a decline may signal reputation issues or decreasing relevance

questions.question: Explain the difference between brand voice and brand tone, and describe how a single brand would express the same voice with different tones in a product launch post vs. a customer complaint response.
questions.answer: Brand voice is the consistent personality of the brand across all communications (e.g., confident, warm, witty). Brand tone is the emotional variation applied to that voice depending on context. For a brand with a warm, confident voice: a product launch post would use an excited, celebratory tone ('We've been working on something special, and today we finally get to share it with you!'), while a customer complaint response would use an empathetic, solution-focused tone ('We hear you, and we're sorry this happened. Here's what we're doing to fix it right now.'). The underlying personality (warm, direct, human) remains constant; the emotional register changes.

questions.question: What is a messaging hierarchy, and why is it more useful than a single tagline for guiding content creation?
questions.answer: A messaging hierarchy is a nested framework that includes mission statement, tagline, value propositions, and proof points, organized from most abstract to most specific. It is more useful than a single tagline because a tagline captures the brand essence in a few words but provides no guidance for specific content decisions. A messaging hierarchy gives content creators a clear structure: every piece of content should support a value proposition, which supports the tagline, which supports the mission. This ensures all content is strategically aligned while allowing variety in execution.

rubric.key: Explains how hierarchy guides content creation

questions.question: Describe Simon Sinek's Golden Circle framework and explain how a digital communications professional would apply it to restructure an organization's 'About Us' web page.
questions.answer: The Golden Circle has three layers: Why (the organization's purpose and belief), How (the unique process or approach), and What (the products or services). Most organizations communicate from outside in (What first), but more compelling communication starts with Why. To restructure an About Us page: lead with the organization's purpose and the problem they exist to solve (Why), then describe their unique approach (How), and finally list their products or services (What). This structure creates an emotional connection before presenting commercial information, making the page more engaging and memorable.

rubric.key: Applies framework to About Us page specifically

questions.question: Define share of voice and explain how a decrease in share of voice combined with an increase in branded search volume should be interpreted.
questions.answer: Share of voice is the percentage of total industry conversation or visibility that your brand occupies across channels. A decrease in share of voice combined with an increase in branded search volume likely means that while more individuals are specifically seeking out the brand (positive brand awareness growth), the overall industry conversation is growing faster, with competitors gaining proportionally more visibility. This combination suggests the brand has strong loyalist engagement but is losing ground in broader market awareness. The recommendation would be to invest in top-of-funnel awareness activities while maintaining the loyalty drivers that are generating the branded search growth.

major_assignments.title: Complete Brand Identity and Communications System
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive brand identity and communications system for a real startup, nonprofit, or student organization. The deliverable includes brand strategy, visual identity, voice and messaging framework, and a sample content campaign demonstrating the system in action.
objectives: Define brand positioning, personality, and target audience through research
Create a visual identity system with digital brand guidelines
Develop a voice document and messaging hierarchy
Produce sample content demonstrating the system across three channels
deliverables: Brand strategy document (4-5 pages) with positioning, personality, and audience analysis
Visual brand guidelines (8-10 pages) covering logo, color, typography, imagery, and templates
Voice and messaging framework with personality attributes, guidelines, and channel examples
Sample content campaign: three content pieces across different channels applying the brand system

criteria: Positioning is distinct and defensible
Target audience is clearly defined with supporting research
Brand personality is well-articulated with clear attributes
Strategy aligns with the organization's actual goals and market position
Visual system is cohesive, professional, and appropriate for the audience
Guidelines are comprehensive enough for someone else to execute consistently
Accessibility standards are met in all color and typography choices
Templates demonstrate practical application across digital formats
Voice attributes are distinctive and differentiating
Messaging hierarchy is complete from mission to proof points
Do/don't guidelines with examples make the voice actionable
Channel adaptation guidelines show how voice flexes across contexts
Content pieces clearly apply the visual and verbal brand system
Each piece is platform-native and professionally executed
Brand consistency is evident across all three pieces
Content demonstrates how the system works in practice, not just theory
Strategic brand positioning grounded in research
Professional visual identity system with comprehensive guidelines
Distinctive voice and messaging framework with actionable guidelines
Sample content demonstrating the system in convincing real-world application

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on brand strategy, identity, and measurement concepts

weekly_exercises.description: Weekly brand analysis and creation exercises

major_assignment.description: Complete brand identity and communications system project

midterm_examination.description: Written examination on brand strategy and identity theory

final_presentation.description: Final brand system presentation with professional critique

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Brand workshops and critique sessions require participation. More than 3 absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Brand system deliverables submitted more than 5 days late receive no credit.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer feedback on brand work is encouraged and structured. All graded deliverables are individual. Sharing brand system documents or design files between students is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All brand work must be original. Visual assets must be original designs or properly licensed. Copying existing brand systems and presenting them as original is plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Figma or Canva Pro for brand design, Google Docs for documentation, and access to social listening tools (demos provided). A laptop with reliable internet is required.
